speaker: interviewer
text: So you were telling me you were back in your hometown. And the neighbors were happy to see you. And were there American soldiers in the town as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: The American soldiers were in the town. They were with anti-aircraft. That's the outfit where my husband was in at that time. And-- but they were only there for not to-- to guard or anything like that. They had to occupy some of the homes there. For they were traveling through there for some reason. I don't know why they were there. There was nothing to be guarded. But it was a whole ninth of something. He could tell you more about that. I am not too familiar with those terms there. But they were stationed there. And he was the captain's chief driver.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was the captain's--

speaker: interviewee
text: Chief driver. And he was the bugle boy. He blew the bugle in the morning and at night.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Henry. Henry Lentini. Yeah. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one night, I was down in my cousin's house. And they happened to march by there. And they started to talk, you know, with their little broken German this and that. And-- and I had met him then one time, you know? I wasn't interested in boys. It was not my thing at that time. My hair wasn't grown in yet or anything like that. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a few weeks later, a girlfriend, next door neighbor, a girlfriend, she was-- she had met the soldier there. And they were going out a little bit here and there. There was nowhere to go really. Just going [INAUDIBLE], but walking around there in the areas-- they-- you know, not much they could do. But she told me he was coming. And we were sitting outside and talking-- young girls, you know. My sister was looking out the window there. And I see them around the corner coming there. And he was there-- there again. And I met him there the second time. Let's just ask him in-- my sister-- my sister Bloomy, the one who died. She was older than me. She asked him in. And we befriended [? there, ?] you know. We could talk. And he came in again. He brought some oranges one time. He brought the kids some, the little ones-- you know, [? Fred's-- ?] and the little ones. [? Anne ?] was small at that time. Even my sister, [? Elise, ?] was only about eight or nine then. And they used to come around once in a while. I was not interested. As a matter of fact, I went to my sister in Marburg. I wanted to go down there for a few days. And she was not feeling good with the kids. Her two-- she had the two children. She wasn't feeling so good. So I went down there for a week. And when I got home, my mother in her front room-- my mother had a picture hanging there. They left-- they didn't take that picture. It was a long picture down.

speaker: interviewer
text: And on that picture was a-- was a little picture photograph of him. And it was something signed underneath-- with love, to Julia, from Hank. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I said to my sister. [INAUDIBLE] crazy or something? Why do you think he is coming here for? She says, he don't want to see me. He wants to come see you. I said, oh, for heaven's sake. I said, how you can communicate to those guys? You know, you can't-- but what he did, because he drove the captain a while, he had a little book with German and English-- the vowels, [INAUDIBLE], and all this. Well, he-- he picked up a few words that way. And that's how we become-- we-- we were friends. And of course, he-- he told me that he fell in love with me. I said, oh, baloney. He says, I did. He said, I did. I knew that you were gonna be the one. I said, how do you know I was gonna go with you? Well, I already knew that if you weren't gonna come with me, I was gonna sign up there forever. I wasn't gonna leave you. I was not gonna leave you. I knew that. So we were going out there for about-- we-- we met in '45, no, in '46. This was '46. So you'd been home for a year already?

speaker: interviewee
text: '46 I was already in. My hair was that long. Yeah, you're right. It was '46 because we got married in-- '40-- what did I say, again? No, it was '45. Because we got married in  '46. We married in '45, sometime in '45. I had known him for eight months, because we got married in March '46. That I know. So-- and I knew him for about eight months then. So I did have the short hair. I know I had short hair. He didn't care about the short hair. He thought-- thought that I looked good in short hair. [LAUGHS] That's what he said then, but yeah. And they had a heck of a time getting-- getting permission to-- to marry anybody like that. The only thing that got me the permission is because I was a Holocaust survivor. And through the government, American government, I got the papers. Today I was gonna show you all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was it so hard to marry him-- why did it make a difference?

speaker: interviewee
text: First, there-- there weren't any Germans. As a matter of fact, they weren't even allowed to go with Germans, work with the Germans, those days. And I-- we had to go-- he had to go to his captain and tell him the-- tell him the whole story, how he felt and this and that. And the captain said, well, thank you, but you have to write some kind of letter. He said, then we have to transfer this to the so and so. He said, this is  not gonna be easy. We have to prove that she's a gypsy and that she was in the camp. But it takes a little doing. He said, you need this paper. You need that people. You know how paper work. In Germany, it's the worst thing [INAUDIBLE]. Although the German authorities didn't have that much to do with us. It was the Americans. See, the Germans had nothing to do with it. I only needed my police record there. And, you know, that I was born there. That's about all they had. It was the government, the American government. And the reason there they didn't want any Nazis in there. You know, I mean, it was just for being precautionary for that-- that particular situation. So we finally got-- we drove trains-- took the shoes off because we ran at night. [INAUDIBLE] so poor-- put the shoe underneath the thing, you know, laid down a little bit, because we had to drive all-- ride all night on the train just to go get the-- some paper [INAUDIBLE]. We had to go for the other side of the-- to the corner, [? that ?] Germany. Woke up in the morning. Somebody took my shoes. It was snowing out there. [LAUGHS] So the Red Cross came on the railroad station. They picked me out of there, and they brought me a pair of shoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there's all those little stories, you know, it's-- you go through. And of course, he was smart. He didn't take his shoes off. I had no intention. You didn't think about it. I didn't think anybody would take my shoes, leave me there in the snow. I had no shoes on. So-- and then got-- that was our last paper. And then, of course, they gave him a week off. We had permission. And I got married in my home town there in the church. I've got the little picture there in the church where we got married. What's the name of the church?

speaker: interviewee
text: Saint-- saint-- saint-- it was [INAUDIBLE] Catholic church-- saint-- not Sebastian, St. Joseph.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your husband is Catholic.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He's Catholic. I am-- I am a Protestant. I never changed. But I did get married-- only for the children sake. I did raise my children Catholic. It's just the opposite what my parents did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you come to the States?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came in the states in '46. And that was in. Everything happened in May, June. Everything happens to [? me ?] in May and June.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it hard to decide to leave Germany and come the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I tell you, it was. I was really hesitating, because this is not an easy thing. I just went through hell. And of course, Germany at that time was still hell. It was right after the war-- really not that long after the war. And my brothers-- because I had to have permission from my older brother. I wasn't even 21. And my brothers [INAUDIBLE], happened to like him. They met him. And so, you know, so they were pretty much-- that he was a nice, easy-going guy. So they weren't too-- too worried about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: But-- and they said, what do you got to lose? If you're not happy there, you just let us know, and we'll send you a ticket. [INAUDIBLE], we'll get you back over here. But if you're happy there, then maybe you got some kind of different life over there. Maybe you could start some different life for yourself. I said, if we all get that chance, I said, we're all gonna do our own thing. And it's true. We did all grow up. We all grew up. But there was a big decision to make, you know? It's-- it's not easy. I wouldn't-- I wouldn't recommend it-- not when you have a family where we went through and left my family and there. And, I mean-- And it's not easy. It's never went away. This is always there. And it's the same thing with my sisters-- or my brothers.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, it's such a tie-- such a bond, such a bind there. It's because what we went through and because this survivor of business and that we're so grateful. And so they grow apart. Other people grow up and grow apart. But we grew still closer. As old as we are, my-- my sister [? Elise, ?] on the phone she says, it's funny. We never cut-- cut from the neighbhor-- [LAUGHS] always-- we were still-- it just makes you closer and stronger for some reason. And I know it did. It did me. And I know it does my family. I don't know about other people. I'm just speaking for my own.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now we're in Biedenkopf. I'm back home again. I met Hank. I went on the boat. I went-- I got to tell you about my boat ride. They-- we were married in the German church-- a little church there. It's six horses and a carriage. And the beer-- there is a brewery in my hometown. And they donated one of those keg of beers. And I borrowed a-- a dress from a lady. I didn't have any. But she had her wedding dress and her little veil. I do have it in the picture. You could see that. It wasn't mine. I borrowed it from the lady.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Hank was in his uniform. And we made a little dinner there what we had, with the few things we had. It was better than what we had in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people give you--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- Oh, we were still on the-- no, we were on-- we got double rations. I still got some-- I want to show you the ration cards. I still have some over there. I should-- they gave us double rations. So we had-- made a little dinner. And we had some friends over.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who gave you double rations? The American government?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- from the government, yeah. It was not the American government. They made the German government. This was German butchers and German bakers, you know, I mean, not the Americans. But the Americans seen to it that we got the double ration because of the survivors from the Holocaust where the German people only had single. But they gave us double, because, you know, we all sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: For the whole time you got double rations? Or just because of the wedding?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. For the whole time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the reaction from the Germans? Did they resent that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They did not resent that at all. No. Some Germans they will tell you today they didn't know it. Some of them did. They didn't know it till after it got publicized.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it really sounds as though your experience-- you had great relations with the Germans before you got taken to the camp and after. And it was just kind of like this--

speaker: interviewee
text: Only with hometown people-- not all of them, just immediately.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the wider society when-- like, before the war when you were traveling with your family? Was there-- did people--

speaker: interviewee
text: We had no-- no reason to. There was-- there was nothing-- you see, I didn't know I was a gypsy. I didn't know I had a Jewish background, that-- that the great grandmothers there got together with the two Jewish boys. I didn't-- we didn't know that.

speaker: interviewer
text: We haven't talked about that on the tape. Do you want to tell me what you found out about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, this is-- this is what they found out. So they said we were-- I think it was 70% German, and the rest was Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you find this out.

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war. After the war. It was all after-- it was all on those papers. It was all after the war. And that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me which papers.

speaker: interviewee
text: The papers we got. I don't have any on me anymore. There was-- of course, we didn't each get one. They sent us a paper that-- that we were-- was in the-- in the-- in the prison paper, that we were so much percentage Jewish and and gypsy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you think that's why you got sent to Auschwitz was the-- was the Jewish blood?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I think the gypsy. Because there were a lot of gypsies in there too. We were more gypsy than-- than Jewish. There was a 70% gypsy. So what the heck? Those German girls must have been-- those gypsy girls much have been pretty [INAUDIBLE]. And I'll tell you, it had to be in the blood line because, you see, my dad was raised that way. And yet, I remember my grandfather was not a gypsy. But my grandmother was too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your grandmother was a gypsy?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandmother was a gypsy, not my grandfather. And they never went with wagons.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did-- how did you know your grandmother was a gypsy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I knew my grandmother was a gypsy. My-- our parents told us. And they lived in the little-- not too far away in Mudersbach, a little town. They had a little farm and things there. My grandma blackest [INAUDIBLE], got a little German outfits on there. She wiggled around in there. And he was-- he was a farmer, my grandpa. She died before him. And I think he got to be 90-something years old. He died just before we went in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you never-- growing up, you didn't really have a feeling from yourself of being different at all--

speaker: interviewee
text: I never thought we were different-- never thought we were different at all-- no reason. I-- I mean, I know my mother was Catholic. And I know my father was a Protestant. And my mother didn't want to go to church with us. I knew that. But that's about it-- you know, not-- till we're-- even my dad, till we were arrested. And they didn't him why we were there. We wanted to go like I told you. They were-- just-- we were gonna straighten up some kind of a [NON-ENGLISH] they called it. And they were just gonna go--

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Over to the papers with him. [NON-ENGLISH] for the race you come from-- [NON-ENGLISH] They're looking through it. You know what  I mean? They are checking out your back-- your family tree.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that point, were you aware of any of the-- the Nazi concerns with racial backgrounds? Was that all part of the popular knowledge? Not for you? You're 14.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not no, nothing really. There was a little political party. There was the Hitler Jugend. There was-- we were not allowed to join it. My mother wouldn't allow-- I told you he wouldn't let us do this and and let us do that. And then with the politics, he said, I got plenty for you to do at home. So-- and I don't think it was the reason. I think they probably just-- maybe my dad did resent this little political-- and I know he worked for the-- for the state there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he work for the Nazis? Or for the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not for the Nazis. I think it was a little [NON-ENGLISH], that he wasn't going along. Maybe they knew that too. See? [INAUDIBLE] they didn't, never said anything. They didn't hate-- [? say ?] they hate us, this and that. The only thing is, you know, when  we went to the store as children, 12 years, 13 years, 12 years old, you had to go-- It's no more good morning or good day. It was all the Heil Hitler. But this Heil Hitler was-- I thought it was with the BDM and the-- and the-- for kids stuff, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: But that thing grew into big, serious stuff, see? And that's why she told me, you don't-- what do you Heil Hitler for? All the kids do it. Forget it. You know? Did you listen to her? Or did you keep doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't want to stick out like a sore thumb. I did it too, to be honest. You know. I mean, don't have-- didn't know the meaning of it. Didn't-- I didn't want to be-- [INAUDIBLE] like peer pressure like today. That's what they call you. You want to get along with the rest of the kids. So sure, I did it. And I used to sneak there once in a while, what is this BDM thing? You know, and I wouldn't dare tell my mother. But I did like take a little peek anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you sit in? Or you just kind of peeked in the windows?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no, they wouldn't let you in, because they didn't have no uniform. You needed to have a uniform. Mothers had to get you their uniforms. The girls had the black skirts and-- and little mustard shirt there with a little black tie there on the necks. And I thought it was kind of cute. You know, when you're young, you don't think any of that. But after then, when we got arrested. And you get picked up. And you get into this world. The [? end isn't-- ?] it's like hell.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did-- has that changed your-- how have your views on the way changed--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'll tell you how it changed me. I tell you, on the end of-- on the middle in all this survival situation there-- people dying daily by the hundreds, by the thousands. My own mother they threw her up there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we wanted to see that, because we-- we talked to that truck-- the guy who picked them up. And we told him, can we have her put on the end. That kapo had talked to that Nazi guy already. We just want to lay her in the back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you did that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he let us do that. And that's all there was to it. He was just-- I mean, instead of throwing her up her, we laid her ourselves in the back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us your mother's name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Johanna. My mother was young. My mother was only 46 years-- had all those children. I told you. And she got married when she was about 16. And I think she had her first baby when she was 15 or 16, around that. It was when-- if I figure out the time-- and my brother Heinrich is the oldest. She was very young, because I was going to ask your one time, you know. And my sister-- my older sister says, you better not. She's going to slap you in the mouth. You better be quiet.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- then later on when I went over there one year, and she had a younger sister. And she was the only one alive yet. And I told her about, you know, how old was my mom. Ah, she said, your mom was young. And said, well, how young? Well, she said, I know one thing. She was not 16. She ran away with your dad. I said, she did? Oh, she said, she was the black sheep. She was always crazy about your father. She was running after your dad. She got [INAUDIBLE] called her back. And she took off with him again. And if I look at the time limit, I think he's about two years older than her. So I said, oh, how interesting-- her name was Lena too. I would love to hear. Next time I come, I want to stay with you for a week. Oh, yeah. So my sister Anna, the oldest-- now, why do you want to hear all that? What-- what do you care about it? I said, I think it's interesting. [LAUGHS] And you know, I never got to-- then the next time, she was dead. I was gonna stay with her for a week. And I wanted to her to tell me all about that. I thought it was so interesting. You know? No wonder she was-- my mother was strict with us-- you know, very strict. She said to us, not with the sex or anything, never mind. Like-- like she said, I got plenty of work to do for you guys here. You don't have to join the BDM. I'll give you BDM. I-- [LAUGHS] it's the kind of thing you have to be home certain hours and had to do our chores. We had to do-- and that way-- of course, you had to be all those children. You had to have something. And she had most of the control, not my dad, because my dad was always busy. And anything went wrong, she had to account for the situation with the boys and girls. So it was a lot of responsible. Now, look at her. Wasn't that a beautiful woman? My dad there. By-- by the time she died, she was 46 years old. My mother looked like she was 65. And I say my mother was born to-- to suffer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, you're 16 years old-- you give birth to 15 children. And you die such a miserable death. I think that was-- not the-- have any-- mother had no life at all. She was happy with a few things, the little things-- the children. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Has that influenced your philosophy of life?

speaker: interviewee
text: That sure did. That sure did. And I am the same way with my children and my grandchildren. My-- sometimes I feel like blowing them all out of it and the troubles that they are there and [? inform ?] them. They can't help it. That's the way it is. It is the way it is. There's nothing I can do. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me about your-- your feelings about religion. Have your experiences in the camps changed anything at all for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the only-- I meant to tell you that. I thought I wasn't capable of loving anymore anybody. And I really didn't think there was a God. It wasn't that much of a deal to me. But I didn't pray anymore, because I didn't think there was any because I always said to myself there can't be God, because the God I was taught wouldn't let that happen-- innocent people-- innocent children. Innocent-- this is-- this is innocent of-- this is-- this is-- it's-- it's a murder thing. It's a terrible thing. And it can't be. And I didn't think I could love anybody. I didn't trust anybody. I didn't trust anybody. Even my husband took a lot. That's why. And he understood this because he knew right away I was in a camp and everything. And he understood that. No, I did not. And he was kind. And it took him a little time. And you know, I have to tell you, it was no big love at first sight. This grew in. You know, we were married over 50 years. It's about 50 years now. And it grows-- it just grew into-- if that's what you call love, then I grew into that. And I had to learn to trust people again that-- and-- and accept that this was a certain time in my life and that would never happen in a life again-- never, never to anyone, not even to the dogs, never mind to human beings, the dignity. Whatever you had, I didn't have that much, because I was too young. When I think about my mother standing in that picture there with her little hands there, raised us not to be in [NON-ENGLISH]. It's just a little something to raised us by-- you know, not in front of your brother to schlep. And this is for the boys. And you're the girls. And this is how we were raised. And here all her teaching was robbed from her in front of hundreds and thousands of people there. Well, if-- my mother was [NON-ENGLISH] right then and there. That I'll tell you. Somebody ripped something out of there-- some artery went or something, because my mother was-- three months later, my mom was dead. She did get the typhoid. My mother had the typhoid. She had the stomach typhoid. That's the way that situation is.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I told you they did put her in the sick block and that-- there-- that kapo who had contact with that Pollack guy-- at the end-- that he knew he was-- he let-- oh, that's-- I mentioned that bench to you. He let her come back to the  park. It was only overnight, because we told him, I said, you know, she's gonna die. Can't she be here while we're-- we're around? We like to be with her.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's how she happen to come to the block for that one night. He got out of there. And that's how she wound up that we put her on the back in the truck. She went-- otherwise, they would have been taken her right from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you so much for sharing with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. OK, here's a family picture. And the first one there on the left is me, which we're gonna call Julia Lentini-- Julia Backer, born Backer, Lentini. And then comes my little brother Fritz-- Fritz Backer. My oldest sister Anna Klein, born a Backer. And that little fella on my mother's lap there is Otto Backer-- my mother, Johanna Backer. Then there's Lena Becker. The one with the polished shoes. Then I got my brother, Vilan drinking coffee, I got my dad sitting right next to him, Ludwig Backer. This is my sister Charlotte sitting right on-- next to my dad. Then we have Ernst Backer up in the corner on the right-hand side. And the last one on the right-hand side is my brother Heinrich Backer. And in the middle there over my sister Lena's is my sister [? Deeni ?] Backer. In the back her is my brother George. And on the left-hand side in a little dark outfit with a cup-- cup of coffee is my brother Carl.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when and where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was taken somewhere in Germany. We were with the wagons out. I don't know. But the year must have been around

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the wagon we used to drive around with in the summertime-- one of the wagons, like I say, we changed every year. But this is the gypsy wagon-- the wagon we traveled in. And if you really look in the back, you see that little [INAUDIBLE] I was telling you about. There is one in the back there. Anyway, the girl here on the end on the right-hand side is my sister Charlotte Backer. And this is a friend of theirs, [? Bubi, ?] a friend of ours-- family friend. Then comes our sister [? Linda ?] next to him. In front of her, it's-- it's Ernst, my brother Ernst Backer. Then there is William, who is turning his face-- William Backer. Right above him with  his hat showing is Otto and my mother Johanna Backer who has Fritz on her arm-- my father Fritz Backer-- my sister Anna next to-- next to her. And right below her is my sister [? Vist ?] turning her head. And next to-- my sister Anna is a friend of the family again. And if you looked real close, you see my dad's hat looking out the window above. That is my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when and where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was taken around the same era at the same time.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany-- all of them in Germany, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Any one of them. OK, now, this is a picture from the day my sister Lena died-- the one who came home from the concentration camp for about three weeks, four weeks. And she died at home. Now, on the right hand is my brother Ernst. Above that is my sister [? Deeni ?] Backer-- Backer-- all the Backers are there. And then my brother Ludwig Backer there-- Ludwig Backer-- then my brother George Backer, my oldest brother Heinrich Backer, and below him on the right is my brother Karl in his short pants. Then there is my sister Charlotte sitting down. Next to her is my baby brother Fritz. And next to him is my older sister Emma Backer, and myself next to that, Julia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he is with the siblings who survived the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Backer. And that the survivors from the 15 children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And father and mother. And I listed them-- that's all the pictures, all the survivors. And I listed them from left to right.
